2: A direct revelation: Jesus Christ has come in the flesh — every spirit that confesses this is from God. The incarnation is not merely one doctrine among others but the watershed Christological claim: the eternal Son's permanent assumption of humanity. The perfect participle (having-come-in-flesh) declares not merely that the incarnation happened but that its results remain — the risen Christ is still the enfleshed Christ, the glorified one still bearing the wounds (John 20:27). This confession is the boundary of genuine Christology and the test of any claimed spiritual experience.
9: A direct revelation: in this the love of God was revealed among us, that God sent his only Son into the world so that we might live through him. The revelation of love is not a general religious insight but a specific historical event: the sending of the monogenes Son (the one-and-only Son, echoing John 3:16). The telos of the sending (that we might live through him) establishes the Son's role as the mediatorial source of eschatological life — not an example of life to imitate but a source of life to receive. All the letter's ethics flow from this gift-logic: received life produces outgoing love.
10: A direct revelation: in this is love, not that we loved God but that he loved us and sent his Son to be the propitiation for our sins. The definition of love is again grounded in the Son-sending, specifically in its atoning dimension. The propitiation (hilasmos) names the specific mechanism by which the love was enacted: not sentimental feeling but wrath-satisfying sacrifice. The prevenience of God's love (not that we loved God but that he loved us) is the Christological datum: love found its form in the cross before anyone had yet responded to it.
14: A direct revelation: the Father has sent his Son to be the Savior of the world. The universal scope of salvation (Savior of the world, not merely of the community or of Israel) is grounded in the Father's specific sending of the Son. The title Savior (soter) carries both Jewish liberation-history resonance (YHWH as Israel's only savior, Isaiah 43:11) and Greco-Roman political resonance (emperors and benefactors called soter in Ephesus). John applies both registers to Christ: he fulfills the divine salvation-monopoly of the OT while surpassing the civic salvation-claims of imperial religion.
1: A direct revelation: the discernment command is necessary precisely because of the reality of Christ — the incarnate Son is the criterion by which every spirit-claim is assessed. The test is Christological (1 John 4:2): the genuine Spirit confesses Christ; false spirits deny him. The "many false prophets" is a sign that the last hour (1 John 2:18) has arrived and that Christ's coming has triggered the eschatological crisis of rival Christological claims. The community needs to discern not despite having Christ but in order to protect their hold on the one in whom their life is grounded.
3: A direct revelation: the spirit that does not confess Jesus (the specific human name, the incarnate person) is identified as the spirit of antichrist. The antichrist's spirit works by dissolving the identity of the incarnate Jesus — separating the historical human Jesus from the divine Christ (Cerinthian Christology) or denying his full incarnation (Docetism). The Spirit from God always points to and confirms the identity of Jesus as the incarnate Son; the spirit of error dismantles this identity — making the Christological confession the single most reliable indicator of a spirit's origin.
4: A direct revelation: the "one who is in you" who is greater than the evil one is Christ (or the Spirit of Christ) dwelling in the community. Christ has already overcome the world (John 16:33) and the evil one (John 12:31-32), and his indwelling presence makes the community participants in his accomplished victory. The community's overcoming is not their own spiritual achievement but the power of the one who dwells in them — a Christological resource against the anti-Christian spirits. Their confidence rests not in themselves but in the greater one who has already won.
5: A revelation of God: the secessionists' worldly origin explains their worldly audience — those who speak from the world's Christology (one that spiritualizes Christ, removes him from flesh, or separates him from the cross) are heard by those whose framework is worldly. The community of Christ, by contrast, speaks the apostolic testimony about the incarnate Christ — which is heard only by those who are from God (1 John 4:6). The division of audience follows the division of origin, which itself follows the Christological division between those who confess the full incarnation and those who deny it.
6: A direct revelation: the apostolic community's message about the incarnate Christ is the content of what "knowing God" makes a person receptive to. The spirit of truth is the Spirit of Christ who inspired the apostolic testimony; the spirit of error is the spirit that distorts or dissolves it. The Christological criterion (who confesses the incarnate Christ) is simultaneously the ecclesiological criterion (who belongs to the apostolic community) and the pneumatological criterion (which spirit is speaking through the teacher). These three discernment tests converge on the single question of Christological confession.
7: A revelation of God: love comes from God — specifically from the God who "so loved the world that he gave his Son" (John 3:16; 1 John 4:9-10). Those born of God through faith in Christ participate in the divine love that constitutes God's character. Knowing God (the God who is love revealed in Christ) produces love because the knower participates in the character of the one they know. The love-community is the community born of the God who loved first in Christ — their love for one another is the extension of God's own love, received through the Son and distributed through the Spirit.
8: A direct revelation: "God is love" is revealed definitively in Christ — the sending of the Son is the form that divine love took in history (1 John 4:9-10). To know God (the God who is love revealed in the cross) and not love is a contradiction: knowing the love-revealing God necessarily includes participation in his character. The one who does not love does not know this God — they may know about a God, but not the God whose essential character is the costly, self-giving love that the incarnation and crucifixion made definitively visible. Love is what God is shown to be in Christ.
11: A revelation of God: the ethical imperative follows from the Christological indicative with logical force (opheilomen: we owe, we are obligated). The community that has received the love of God in Christ (the atoning sacrifice, the gift of life through the Son) is obligated by the gift to extend the same love within the community. The love is not reciprocated to God directly (he needs nothing) but flows horizontally within the community as the Christologically-shaped life takes form in the community's relationships — the love of the Son for his community reproduced in the community's love for one another.
12: A direct revelation: the invisible God becomes visible in the community's love for one another. The God who is love (1 John 4:8) and who was revealed in the sending of the Son (1 John 4:9) now makes himself present in the community that practices the love that Christ defined and modeled. The love's "completion" in the community is the final form of what the incarnation began: God's love that appeared in Christ (1 John 4:9) is made complete when it takes the form of the community's practice of loving one another — the incarnation extended into the community's common life.
13: A direct revelation: the Spirit given by God (through Christ: John 14:16-17; 1 John 15:26; 1 John 16:7) is the indwelling evidence of the mutual abiding. The community in Christ and Christ in the community is verified by the Spirit's presence — making the Trinitarian structure of Christian life explicit: living in God is verified by the Spirit who is given through the Son. The Spirit's presence within the community is the continuing presence of the one who gave him — Christ's own life communicated to the community through the Spirit he sent.
15: A direct revelation: confessing that Jesus is the Son of God is the condition for God's dwelling in the confessor. The mutual indwelling (God in them, they in God) is Christologically conditioned: access to the divine fellowship is through and only through confessing the specific identity of the one through whom God has made himself accessible. The community that confesses and the community that has God living in it are the same community — united by the confession of the one who is himself both the gateway and the destination of the Trinitarian fellowship.
16: A direct revelation: "God is love" as the repeated theological anchor (first in 1 John 4:8) is the Christological summary — the God whose essential character is love has revealed that love definitively in sending the Son. Living in love (the community's practice of the Christ-defined love) is living in God — because love is not a human quality added to the community's Christological relationship with God, but the very character of the God they are in. The community's life of love is its life in God, and its life in God is its life of love — inseparably the same.
17: A direct revelation: "we are like him in this world" — the community is being formed into the likeness of Christ in this world as the anticipation of the full conformity to his image at the parousia (1 John 3:2). Love made complete in the community is Christiformity expressed — the community shaped by the cross-love of Christ becoming increasingly like the one whose cross-love shaped them. The day of judgment confidence flows from this Christological conformity: those who are like him will recognize him and be recognized by him on the day he appears.
18: A revelation of God: the perfect love that drives out fear is the love of God in Christ that has already dealt with the punishment that fear anticipated. Fear of judgment is the appropriate response to undealt-with sin; Christ's atonement is the dealing-with of sin that removes the ground of that fear. Those who are made perfect in love (mature in the reception and expression of the cross-love of God) have no remaining fear because the atonement has removed the penal ground for it. The cross is both the revelation of love and the destruction of fear.
19: A direct revelation: the community's love is derivative and responsive — it exists because God loved first in Christ (1 John 4:9-10). The community cannot manufacture this love independently; it is always secondarily responsive, always a participation in the prior love of God that appeared in the Son's incarnation and atonement. The prevenience of God's love in Christ is the permanent ground of all community love: "We love because he first loved us" is the Christological summary of the letter's entire ethical structure — grace that descends produces love that flows outward.
20: A revelation of God: the visible brother is the test of love for the invisible God — a specifically Christological logic. God became visible in Christ (John 1:14, 18; 1 John 4:9); the community now encounters the invisible God through the visible members of the body of Christ. To hate the visible brother (who is in Christ) is to hate the God who is in Christ — making the claim to love God while hating the brother a self-refuting falsehood. The incarnation has given the community visible form, and love for God necessarily flows through and is expressed in love for that visible community.
21: A direct revelation: Christ's command (John 13:34-35) links love for God to love for the community permanently and inseparably. The community of Christ is not a voluntary association of like-minded individuals but a Christologically constituted body whose internal love is commanded, not optional. The one who loves God (the God who sent the Son) loves the community that the Son has gathered — because the same love that defines God (1 John 4:8) is the love that the Son demonstrated (1 John 3:16) and commanded the community to practice. The two loves are one love, given one form by the incarnate Christ.
